nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on outer reef edges and coral-rich areas of the Great Barrier Reef and surrounding Coral Sea, including Lord Howe Island. Tends to occur at depths of about 2–40 m (most commonly 8–25 m), where small to large midwater aggregations feed on zooplankton above hard coral reef structure.

diet.notes: Planktivorous in the wild, feeding on zooplankton in midwater above the reef. In captivity tends to accept a varied diet of meaty frozen foods (mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, calanus, cyclops, finely chopped seafood) alongside marine flake, micro-pellets, and herbivore preparations. Multiple small feedings per day are generally advised to mirror natural grazing on plankton. Vitamin- and HUFA-enriched foods have been reported to help maintain colouration.

avoidWith: other Pomacentrid damselfish in small tanks

summary: A peaceful schooling damselfish from Australia's Great Barrier Reef, recognisable by its yellow upper body and silver flanks split by a dark diagonal stripe. Generally considered reef-safe and well-suited to small groups in mature reef aquariums.
careNotes:
The Barrier Reef Chromis (Chromis nitida) is found only in the Western Pacific, with its range restricted to eastern Australia — including the Great Barrier Reef and Coral Sea — and Lord Howe Island. In the wild it tends to form small to large midwater aggregations above outer reef edges at depths of roughly 2–40 m, most commonly between 8 and 25 m, where groups rise off the reef to feed on drifting zooplankton.

Adults reach about 8–10 cm in length and are identified by a dark diagonal stripe running from behind the eye to the rear of the dorsal fin, with a yellow upper body and silver-white lower body. The alternate common names Yellowback Puller and Shining Puller both refer to this distinctive two-tone pattern.

This is generally considered an intermediate-level species. A tank of around 200 L with a minimum 120 cm footprint is recommended for a small group. Although minimum sizes as low as 114 L (30 US gallons) are sometimes cited for a single specimen, the Barrier Reef Chromis is a shoaling fish whose conspecific dominance hierarchies — where dominant individuals can persistently harass subordinates — make more modest volumes less suitable when keeping multiple fish. Groups of six or more tend to distribute this social pressure more widely, reducing stress on lower-ranked individuals. A single specimen has also been kept successfully as a dither fish (a bold, active fish added to encourage shy tankmates to come out into the open).

This species has been reported to be susceptible to Uronema marinum, a parasitic ciliate that can cause rapid tissue loss. A quarantine period of at least four weeks before introduction to a display tank is generally advised, alongside a diet enriched with vitamins and HUFAs (highly unsaturated fatty acids), which has been reported to support colouration and overall condition.

Reproduction in home aquaria has been documented but is uncommon. The Barrier Reef Chromis is oviparous (egg-laying) with distinct pair bonding; demersal eggs (which adhere to hard substrate) are deposited on the reef and guarded and aerated by the male. Rearing the small pelagic larvae — which hatch and drift with the current before settling — is considered difficult under home aquarium conditions.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pair bonding. Eggs are demersal and adhere to hard substrate; males guard and aerate the clutch. Spawning in home aquaria has been reported but is uncommon, and captive rearing of the small pelagic larvae is considered difficult.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous; pair bonding documented. Demersal adhesive eggs guarded and aerated by the male. Captive spawning is uncommon and rearing the small pelagic larvae is considered difficult.
